label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the question and the paragraph carefully. The question is asking what the Indians believed according to the letter they wrote. The answer choices are (A) it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling, (B) they were being insulted by the offer, (C) they knew more about science than the officials, (D) they had better way of educating young men. First, I'll go through the letter to understand the main points. The letter says that the Indians were invited to send their boys to William and Mary College, but they refused. They acknowledged the officials' good intentions and thanked them. However, the Indians pointed out that different nations have different ways of looking at things, and they might not agree with the officials' ideas of education. They then mention that some of their young people had gone to colleges in the northern provinces, learned "all your sciences," but when they came back, they were "bad manners, ignorant of every means of living in the woods-they were totally good for nothing." Finally, they offer to educate the sons of the gentlemen of Virginia in their ways. The key points to consider here are the Indians' belief in their own education system versus the one
